title: Rusted iron resolve
text: Cynical but fair pressure; lasting injuries matter; no free destiny. Wording stays dry and practical.
examples: the pier does not care who you were

title: Reflective river curiosity
text: Soft danger, community ties, mysteries that invite looking closer rather than grinding levels.

title: Wary ethical conflict
text: Polite surfaces over sharp debts; reputation can hurt more than blades.
examples: a smile that costs three favors

title: Bleak system weather
text: Relentless logistics and UI pressure; power is earned in mud and menus, never gifted.
examples: quest complete means you get to keep breathing

title: Cynical pragmatism
text: Another-world start without lovey or free OP framing; practical problem-solving, keep an edge.

title: Mundane isekai compound
text: Other-world life with one weak seed skill that compounds through use and risk—not chosen-one prophecy.

title: Rationed harbor noir
text: Transmigration into dock labor and debt; former desk life is a liability, not a superpower.
examples: your old keyboard muscle memory files wrong

title: Sky-broke system apocalypse
text: After the sky broke, status windows arrived; training-heavy skills, political scavengers, no free spikes.

title: Sect politics first
text: Cultivation is slow and costly; face and debts decide access more than raw level numbers.
examples: rank without face is empty tea

title: Subtle blue-window system
text: Diegetic blue panes that appear sparingly; track rank and risk XP without turning prose into a spreadsheet.
examples: dim rank tick after a real mistake

title: Cold quest-log interface
text: A cold quest log that updates late and lies by omission; players must verify with eyes.

title: Cultivation status pane
text: A spare cultivation pane: bottleneck notes, resource cost, no instant breakthrough buttons.

title: Diegetic omen prompts
text: No hard UI—omens, body tells, and mentor phrases act as the system voice.
examples: scar itches before a bad bargain

title: Lasting injuries over delete
text: Downed not deleted; limbs and stamina carry scars that change play.

title: Permadeath threat, rare save
text: Death is real enough to scare; rare story salvage only through costly alliances, never free reload.
examples: faction buys your body, not your pride

title: Community setback death
text: Death is rare; setbacks cost time, XP, and pride while the village helps recovery.
examples: week of light work after a fall

title: Scarce useful loot
text: Loot is scarce and mostly practical—rope, boots, permits—not rainbow epics every turn.

title: Chits and permits as treasure
text: Paper rights and ration chits matter more than gold; forged papers are high-risk loot.

title: Risk-multiplied practice XP
text: Practice grants low XP; contested or life-risk use multiplies; soft cap after mid ranks until mentor breakthrough.

title: F-to-S rank ladder
text: Letter ranks with rising XP thresholds; each rank above F adds a modest domain check bonus.

title: Level XP linear-plus
text: Levels 1–10 with XP_to_next rising slowly; soft cap mid-curve; small % effect bumps per level.

title: Desk death to dock wake
text: Wake on a ration pier after a dull desk death; no prophecy, just work tickets and wet boots.
examples: your hands know keyboards, not rope

title: Sky-crack first window
text: First scene: sky fractures, a window appears, and the nearest practical problem is not a dragon—it is food and shelter.
examples: tutorial marker over a broken pantry

title: Ferry morning mystery
text: Morning fog on the ferry; a missing crate or person starts a soft mystery without a world-ending clock.
examples: wet footprint that is not the ferryman's

title: Outer court arrival
text: Arrive as an outer-court guest with borrowed manners; one wrong bow is the first conflict.

title: Relative rank NPCs
text: NPCs use relative ranks to the player; specialists exist, elites are rare and named.
examples: dock boss D-rank social, C-rank brawl

title: Some trained, many ordinary
text: Most people are ordinary; a few specialists carry clear skill ranks the player can notice.

title: Training-heavy growth
text: Skills want mentors, drills, and risk—not passive AFK gain. Wording stresses practice loops.

title: One-skill seed frame
text: Exactly one weak seed domain; no second combat toolkit at start; fiction tracks rank; math lives on growth_math.
examples: domain chosen later, never default weather

title: Community milestone XP
text: Skill XP from helping the village and finishing social milestones, not monster tallies.
